literal: non-being, absence, negation; from a- (privative) + bhāva 'being'
invariant_definition: *Abhāva* names absence treated as itself an item to be reckoned with — not merely a privation of cognition but a genuine cognitive object. Vaiśeṣika makes it the seventh *padārtha* alongside the six *bhāva-padārthas*; Mīmāṃsā gives it the status of an independent *pramāṇa* (*anupalabdhi*) to explain how one knows that something is not there. The technical apparatus distinguishes four varieties: *prāgabhāva* (prior absence — the absence of the pot before it is made), *pradhvaṃsābhāva* (posterior absence — the absence of the pot after it is broken), *anyonyābhāva* (mutual absence — the absence of pot-ness in cloth), and *atyantābhāva* (absolute absence — the absence of horns in a hare). Vedānta is split: realists (Madhva, Rāmānuja) take *abhāva* seriously as a real condition; Advaita admits it only at the *vyāvahārika* level, since at the *pāramārthika* level only non-dual *brahman* is.

per_school.definition: *Abhāva* is the seventh category, partitioned into the four classical varieties. Its mode of knowing is debated: some accept *anupalabdhi*; others reduce *abhāva*-cognition to perception of a bare *adhikaraṇa* in its 'absence-qualified' state. Either way, absence is an objective feature of the world, not a mere failure to cognize.

Kumārila treats *anupalabdhi* as a sixth *pramāṇa*: the cognition of *abhāva* through non-apprehension of what would have been apprehended had it been present. The doctrine is essential to Bhāṭṭa epistemology and to the realist defense of inference and verbal testimony.

Prabhākara denies *abhāva* the status of an independent category or *pramāṇa*: what is taken to be cognition of absence is cognition of the bare locus, *kevalādhikaraṇa-jñāna*. The contrast with Bhāṭṭa is one of the school-defining disputes within Mīmāṃsā.

At *vyavahāra* Advaita accepts the *abhāva*-vocabulary and works with Mīmāṃsā's *anupalabdhi*. At *paramārtha* the very framework dissolves: there is no second item whose non-presence could be reckoned. The Advaitic point against the Naiyāyika is that absence cannot itself be a stable categorial entity if the categories themselves are *anirvacanīya*.

Madhva makes *abhāva* real and important: the irreducible differences (*pañca-bheda*) require that absences (e.g. of identity between *jīva* and Hari) be genuinely there in the world, not merely a way of speaking. Sarvajña-Ātman's and other Advaitins' attempts to soften *abhāva* are accordingly resisted.

Nāgārjuna treats *abhāva* in parallel with *bhāva*: neither obtains *svabhāvataḥ*; cessation is not the becoming-real of a non-existent absence. The *Svabhāva-parīkṣā* explicitly attacks the assumption that *abhāva* could underwrite an account of cessation.

translator_note: 'Absence' is the standard rendering, but 'non-being' is sometimes called for in cosmological or ontological contexts. The four-fold typology should be preserved untranslated on first occurrence and glossed inline; English 'absence' alone cannot carry *prāgabhāva* vs *atyantābhāva*.
